titleStmt.title: Automating Interlinear Translation of Ancient Greek Texts: A Digital Humanities Approach to Biblical Translation

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon
Match where technical expertise is helpful.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Interlinear translation represents a highly specialized approach to translation that prioritizes preserving the original syntactic structure over natural fluency (Shuttleworth / Cowie 2014). In this method, target language words are arranged directly beneath their source counterparts, creating a word-by-word correspondence that serves as a crucial tool for scholars studying ancient texts. While conventional machine translation systems often reorder words to produce more natural output, interlinear translation maintains the source text's structure, making it particularly valuable for understanding Ancient Greek's complex morphological system and its relationship to modern languages. This approach holds special significance in biblical and classical scholarship, where precise analysis of the original text's structure is essential. Despite being considered by some scholars as the archetypal form of translation (Benjamin 1923/2000), interlinear translation has remained largely unexplored when it comes to automated translation in digital humanities research.

Our study presents the first systematic investigation of automated interlinear translation from Ancient Greek, using the New Testament (Nestle et al. 2012) as a case study. We demonstrate how modern machine learning techniques can assist scholars in creating interlinear translations utilizing morphological metadata. This research bridges the gap between traditional philological methods and contemporary computational approaches.

This submission presents three main contributions. First, we present the first systematic approach to automating interlinear translation using modern machine learning methods. We evaluate four base models -- PhilTa, GreTa (Riemenschneider / Frank 2023), and mT5 (Xue et al. 2020) (in two sizes) -- across 144 experimental scenarios, providing comprehensive insights into the task's feasibility. Second, we construct a novel word-level-aligned parallel corpus of the Greek New Testament with interlinear translations in English and Polish, based on data from BibleHub (BibleHub n.d.) and Oblubienica (Oblubienica n.d.). Finally, our experiments demonstrate that incorporating morphological information in low-resource settings significantly improves translation quality, with proper morphological tag encoding yielding improvements of 38% for Polish and 35% for English over the baseline. We make the resources developed as part of this work (parallel corpus, training code, and fine-tuned models) publicly available at github.com/mrapacz/interlinear-translation.

Our work bridges multiple research domains: machine translation of ancient texts, which remains largely unexplored with only a few dedicated models like GreTa and PhilTa (Riemenschneider / Frank 2023); biblical translation studies, which typically focus on modern language pairs rather than original ancient sources; and automated interlinear translation, which has not been previously attempted despite its importance in classical scholarship. With Ancient Greek having fewer than 0.1M training pairs available in standard parallel corpora, this research also contributes to our understanding of low-resource neural machine translation, where models typically achieve BLEU scores between 4 and 15 in zero-shot scenarios (Zhang et al. 2020).

div.head: Dataset Preparation
div.p: We developed two parallel corpora comprising interlinear translations of the Greek New Testament: one into Polish (from Oblubienica) and one into English (from BibleHub). The corpora utilize different textual editions, with Oblubienica following NA28 and BibleHub merging NA27 with other critical editions. The final aligned dataset contains 7,940 verses with 137,323 Greek words, 133,581 Polish words, and 185,722 English words. Each Greek word is maintained in two forms: with diacritics (following BibleHub's spelling) and normalized (stripped of diacritics and converted to lowercase). The dataset is further accompanied by two distinct sets of morphological tags (one from BibleHub and one from Oblubienica).

To ensure consistency across translations, we implemented an alignment process that matched words between the two versions. This process successfully aligned over 99% of words, with the remaining unmatched words being excluded to maintain data quality.

div.head: Base Models
div.p: We evaluated four base models:

list.item: GreTa: T5-base variant pre-trained specifically on Ancient Greek texts,
PhilTa: T5-base variant pre-trained on Ancient Greek, Latin and English,
mT5-base and mT5-large: Pre-trained on mC4 corpus covering 101 languages including English and Polish, though notably excluding Ancient Greek.

div.p: These models were chosen to represent different approaches to language understanding: specialized ancient language models ( GreTa, PhilTa), and modern multilingual models ( mT5 variants). We included mT5-large to investigate whether increased model capacity through additional parameters would improve translation performance.

div.head: Morphological Encoding
div.p: To handle Ancient Greek's rich morphology, we tested five approaches for including its grammatical information in our translation models:

list.item: Baseline: No morphological information, providing a control scenario.
Tags Within Text: Here morphological tags are encoded as part of model’s textual input alongside words using special marker tokens as delimiters.

Morphological Embeddings: Three approaches using dedicated embedding layers, varying how text and tag information was combined for the model's encoder:
Sum ( emb-sum): Direct addition of text and tag embeddings.
Autoencoder ( emb-auto): Compression and decompression of tag embeddings before addition.
Concatenation ( emb-concat): Word and tag embeddings are concatenated.

div.head: Other Test Variables
div.p: Beyond the encoding methods, we also tested whether text preprocessing (with or without diacritics and casing) and morphological tag set choice (sourced from BibleHub or Oblubienica) would affect the translation quality.

div.head: Total Experiments
div.p: In total, we evaluated 144 unique configurations. Each configuration involved fine-tuning a model for the interlinear translation task, systematically varying: target language (2 options), base model (4 options), morphological tag encoding (5 approaches), text preprocessing (2 strategies), and tag set (2 options).

div.head: Evaluation
div.p: To assess translation quality, we employed two complementary metrics: BLEU (Papineni et al. 2002), which measure exact word matches and sequence accuracy, and SemScore (Aynetdinov / Akbik 2024), which evaluates semantic similarity using the all-mpnet-base-v2 language model (Sentence Transformers n.d.). This dual approach helps capture both lexical accuracy and meaning preservation, particularly important for translations of ancient texts where word-for-word correspondence may not always indicate the best translation. In evaluation process, we removed separator tokens from the output sequences before comparing with references to ensure fair evaluation without artificially rewarding proper output formatting.

div.head: Results
div.p: Our experiments revealed several key findings.

div.head: Overall Translation Feasibility
div.p: Our experiments demonstrated the feasibility of automated interlinear translation, achieving SemScore values above 0.8 and BLEU scores exceeding 60 for both languages. While both languages received comparable top results, Polish translations showed more variability, with approximately 40% of experiments not surpassing a BLEU score of 2. The correlation between SemScore and BLEU was stronger for English (r=0.97) than Polish (r=0.89), suggesting that BLEU scores may not fully capture translation quality for morphologically complex, inflectional languages.

div.head: Morphological Encoding Impact
div.p: Morphologically enhanced models outperformed baselines by approximately 35-38% for both Polish (59.33 vs 42.92) and English (60.40 vs 44.67). Among morphological encoding strategies, the embedding-based approaches emb-auto and emb-sum consistently delivered the strongest performance, significantly outperforming text-based tag incorporation. In contrast, the emb-concat strategy, while also embedding-based, performed worse than the baseline on average — potentially due to compression disrupting pre-trained representations — though it demonstrated a capacity for high scores in specific instances (up to 55.55 for Polish and 55.93 for English).

div.head: Base Model Comparison
div.p: In terms of base model performance, PhilTa achieved state-of-the-art performance for English translations (BLEU 60.40), while mT5-large performed best for Polish translations (BLEU 59.33). For Polish translations, mT5-large's result significantly outperformed all other models (p < 0.01). For English, PhilTa's scores were significantly better than GreTa’s and mT5-base’s (p < 0.01), though not significantly different from mT5-large (p = 0.46).

We went further ahead to compare how much data is needed for the two top-performing models ( Philta, mT5-large) to get proper performance on the English translation task. In this analysis, PhilTa demonstrated remarkable efficiency in low-resource scenarios, achieving BLEU scores between 36.20-43.52 with just 10% of the corpus (794 verses) used for training. In contrast, mT5-large showed instability with smaller datasets, requiring the full training set to match PhilTa's performance. This suggests that targeted pre-training on Ancient Greek can compensate for model size in this specialized translation task.

div.head: Impact of Preprocessing and Tag Set Choice
div.p: Text preprocessing strategies (preserving vs. removing diacritics) showed no statistically significant differences in translation performance for either language (p > 0.4). Similarly, the choice of morphological tag sets (Bible Hub vs. Oblubienica) had no significant impact (p > 0.07), indicating that the encoding strategy matters more than the specific tag set used. The poor performance of concatenation-based embedding approaches suggests that maintaining pre-trained representations is crucial for effective translation.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: Our work demonstrates the effectiveness of neural approaches in interlinear translation of classic texts. The inclusion of morphological metadata significantly improves translation quality, challenging the notion that pre-trained transformers cannot utilize such information effectively. The superior performance of models pre-trained on both source and target languages suggests that developing similar models for other language pairs could yield even better results.

The research revealed interesting differences in correlations of evaluation metrics for the two languages (r=0.97 for English, r=0.89 for Polish), highlighting potential limitations in using BLEU for morphologically complex languages. While models trained with preserved diacritics showed numerically better results, these differences weren't statistically significant. Similarly, the choice of morphological tag sets had minimal impact across both target languages.

Our study has several limitations worth noting. The exclusive use of the New Testament, while providing a consistent dataset, may limit generalizability across different textual genres. There's also potential bias in the models, which may have encountered biblical texts during pre-training.

The automation of interlinear translations demonstrated in this research opens new possibilities for making ancient texts more accessible to broader audiences. While traditionally, interlinear translations have been limited to select texts and languages due to the intensive manual effort required, our approach could facilitate their creation for previously uncovered language pairs and texts. This democratization of access to ancient texts could particularly benefit students, researchers, and enthusiasts who lack extensive training in ancient languages, enabling them to engage more directly with historical sources while still maintaining a connection to the original text structure.

listBibl.head: Bibliography
listBibl.bibl: Aynetdinov, Ansar / Alan Akbik (2024): "SemScore: Automated evaluation of instruction-tuned llms based on semantic textual similarity". arXiv:2401.17072 < https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.17072> [07.05.2025].
Benjamin, Walter (1923/2000): "The task of the translator", in: Venuti, Lawrence / Mona Baker (eds.): The Translation Studies Reader. London: Routledge.

Nestle, Eberhard / Barbara Aland / Kurt Aland / Holger Strutwolf / Universität Münster Institut für Neutestamentliche Textforschung (2012): Novum Testamentum Graece (Na28): Nestle-Aland 28th Edition. Stuttgart: Deutsche Bibelgesellschaft.

Papineni, Kishore / Salim Roukos / Todd Ward / Wei-Jing Zhu (2002): "Bleu: a Method for Automatic Evaluation of Machine Translation", in: Association for Computational Linguistics (ed.): Proceedings of the 40th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, USA: 311–318.

Riemenschneider, Frederick / Anette Frank (2023): "Exploring Large Language Models for Classical Philology", in: Association for Computational Linguistics (ed.): Proceedings of the 61st Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers), Toronto, Canada: 15181–15199.
Shuttleworth, Mark / Moira Cowie (2014): Dictionary of Translation Studies. London: Routledge.

Xue, Linting / Noah Constant / Adam Roberts / Mihir Kale / Rami Al-Rfou / Aditya Siddhant / Aditya Barua / Colin Raffel (2020): "mt5: A massively multilingual pre-trained text-to-text transformer". arXiv:2010.11934 < https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11934> [07.05.2025].

Zhang, Biao / Philip Williams / Ivan Titov / Rico Sennrich (2020): "Improving massively multilingual neural machine translation and zero-shot translation", in: Association for Computational Linguistics (ed.): Proceedings of the 58th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics, Online: 1628–1639.
